---
title: "The Foundation: Data Cleansing"
author: "David Martinez"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
execute: 
  warning: false
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
```

## Overview
In the real world, data is rarely clean. Before any algorithm can be trained or any graph can be plotted, raw data must be wrestled into a usable format. Across my portfolio, data cleansing has been the most critical—and often most time-consuming—step of the analytical process.

**My approach to data wrangling involves:**

**Standardizing Missing Values:** Identifying and normalizing inconsistent null placeholders (like -999, blank strings, or "N/A") into standard formats so mathematical functions work correctly.

**Format Transformation:** Translating highly complex text data (like survey responses) into machine-readable numeric and binary formats using label encoding and one-hot encoding.

**Preventing Data Leakage:** Rigorously auditing datasets before modeling to remove any columns that might artificially give a machine learning algorithm the “answer key” to the problem it is trying to solve.


## 1. Flight Delays: Standardizing Missing Values
The raw JSON flight dataset contained "missing" data in several breaking formats. I mapped all these anomalies (-999, "", "n/a") to consistent np.nan values so pandas could process the math correctly without skewing the averages.

```{python}
#| eval: false
# Standardizing various missing data types into a single format
df['num_of_delays_carrier'] = pd.to_numeric(df['num_of_delays_carrier'], errors='coerce')
df['month'] = df['month'].replace('n/a', np.nan)
df['airport_name'] = df['airport_name'].replace('', np.nan)

# Replacing arbitrary numerical placeholders
df = df.replace(-999.0, np.nan)
```

## 2. Housing ML: Preventing Data Leakage
When prepping the Denver housing dataset for predictive modeling, the biggest threat was "data leakage." I explicitly stripped out columns like yrbuilt (the exact year built) and parcel (an arbitrary ID). If left in, the model would cheat and score 100% simply by reading the year.

```{python}
#| eval: false
# Dropping columns that leak the target variable or are useless IDs
X = df.drop(columns=['before1980', 'yrbuilt', 'parcel'])
y = df['before1980']
```

## 3. Star Wars Survey: Format Transformation
Survey data is notoriously difficult to process because human answers are text-heavy. I mapped paragraph-long headers to concise names, translated text-based categorical ranges (like ages and incomes) into usable numbers, and transformed 14 separate character favorability columns into a 1-5 ordinal scale.
